did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes Attn-UNTER, a hierarchical transformer-based deep learning model for skin lesion segmentation. It utilizes Vision Transformers (ViT), Convolutional Block Attention Modules (CBAM), and attention-guided skip connections to segment skin lesions from dermoscopic images using the ISIC-2017 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new deep learning model, Attn-UNTER, for skin lesion segmentation. It details the architecture (Vision Transformer, CBAM, attention-guided skip connections), describes the methodology (ISIC-2017 dataset, data augmentation, training with AdamW optimizer), and reports quantitative results (Dice score 89.05%, Jaccard Index 81.72%) comparing it to state-of-the-art methods. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICECTE 2026), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Attn-UNTER: Attention-Guided Hierarchical Transformer for Skin Lesion Segmentation
journal: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Electrical, Computer & Telecommunication Engineering (ICECTE 2026)
brief_summary: This study proposes Attn-UNTER, a hierarchical transformer-based deep learning model designed for automatic skin lesion segmentation from dermoscopic images. The architecture integrates Vision Transformers (ViT) with Convolutional Block Attention Modules (CBAM) and attention-guided skip connections to balance global contextual information with fine-grained local details. The model was evaluated on the publicly available ISIC-2017 dataset, utilizing data augmentation and a hybrid loss function combining binary cross-entropy and Dice loss. Experimental results demonstrate that Attn-UNTER outperforms state-of-the-art CNN and transformer-based methods, achieving a Dice score of 89.05% and a Jaccard Index of 81.72%. These findings suggest that the proposed method effectively improves segmentation accuracy and boundary precision, supporting potential applications in early melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study performs binary segmentation of skin lesions using the ISIC-2017 dataset. While the task is binary (lesion vs. background), the paper highlights 'melanoma' as the primary clinical context and motivation for the work, noting its role in early diagnosis and mortality statistics. No specific histological subtypes (e.g., BCC, SCC, nevus) were segmented or reported as distinct classes in the experiments.

all_task_types.target: Binary segmentation mask of skin lesions (lesion vs. background)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary segmentation of skin lesions from dermoscopic images using the ISIC-2017 dataset. The model predicts pixel-wise masks to delineate lesion boundaries, evaluated using metrics such as Dice Coefficient (89.05%), Jaccard Index (81.72%), and Hausdorff Distance (4.21).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A publicly accessible dataset containing dermoscopic image pairings (original images and ground truth masks) for skin lesion segmentation. Accessed via Kaggle.

use.training: Used for training the Attn-UNTER model (75% of the dataset).
use.validation: Used for validation (25% of the dataset) to determine the best-performing checkpoint.
use.testing: Not explicitly reported as a separate test set; the paper reports results on the dataset generally, likely using the validation split or the full dataset for final evaluation metrics.
use.other: Used for ablation studies to evaluate the impact of CBAM modules and attention-guided skip connections.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons with state-of-the-art methods were based on direct metric values (Dice score, Jaccard Index, etc.) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC-2017.notes: The proposed Attn-UNTER model outperformed state-of-the-art methods including Attention U-Net, SegFormer, TransUNet, DAGAN, and Hybrid Deep Learning. Ablation studies showed that removing both CBAM and attention-guided skip connections reduced performance to a Dice score of 87.43% and Jaccard index of 79.62%, confirming the contribution of these components.

architecture_details.patch_processing: Images divided into patches, embedded, and processed with transformer

training_details.loss_function: Hybrid loss: 0.5 * Dice Loss + 0.5 * Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)

classification_or_output_head.description: High-resolution segmentation masks produced by decoder

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing input dermoscopic images to 112x112 pixels
Data augmentation including flip (horizontal and vertical), rotation, Gaussian blur, and brightness adjustments

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random flips in both horizontal and vertical directions.

conclusions: The proposed Attn-UNTER model, which combines Vision Transformers with Convolutional Block Attention Modules (CBAM) and attention-guided skip connections, outperforms state-of-the-art CNN and transformer-based segmentation methods.

Attn-UNTER achieved a Dice score of 89.05%, a Jaccard Index of 81.72%, an accuracy of 96.67%, a precision of 92.23%, a recall of 88.48%, an F1 score of 90.24%, and a Hausdorff distance of 4.21 on the ISIC-2017 dataset.

Ablation studies confirmed that both CBAM modules and attention-guided skip connections significantly contribute to segmentation performance; removing both reduced the Dice score to 87.43% and Jaccard index to 79.62%.

The hybrid design of Attn-UNTER effectively balances local feature extraction from CNNs with global contextual knowledge from Transformers, making it robust to variations in texture, size, and illumination.
Attn-UNTER achieves a favorable balance between computational efficiency and model capacity, having 60.53M parameters and 18.31G FLOPs compared to larger transformer-based models like CoTr and SETR.
The findings suggest that Attn-UNTER has the potential to assist clinicians in early melanoma detection and improve patient outcomes by providing accurate lesion boundary delineation.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC-2017 dataset for both training and validation, with no external validation on independent datasets, limiting the assessment of generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The input resolution is restricted to 112x112 pixels to balance performance and GPU memory, which may compromise the preservation of fine-grained boundary details critical for precise lesion delineation.
The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the comparison with state-of-the-art methods, relying solely on direct metric comparisons.
The model was trained on only 1,500 images (75% of the dataset) with a small batch size of 8, which may increase the risk of overfitting despite the use of data augmentation.

The work is limited to binary segmentation of skin lesions; it does not address multi-class classification or segmentation of specific lesion subtypes (e.g., melanoma vs. nevus), which limits direct clinical utility for differential diagnosis.
The authors acknowledge that the current architecture is not optimized for real-time clinical applications and propose lightweight versions for future work, indicating current deployment constraints.
